tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is accurate recognition and documentation of cluster headache (familial context may be noted but does not change the primary diagnosis).

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies the condition as cluster headache and explicitly uses the term "cluster headache" when explaining the diagnosis.

Elicits key cluster headache features: severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration (15–180 minutes), attack frequency (up to multiple/day), circadian/seasonal pattern, and ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, miosis/ptosis) or restlessness/agitation.

Assesses red flags and secondary headache features that would change management (e.g., sudden thunderclap onset, new neurologic deficit, meningismus/fever, new headache after age 50, immunosuppression/cancer, pregnancy/postpartum, trauma, progressive pattern change).

Records an appropriate differential diagnosis (at minimum: migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, paroxysmal hemicrania/SUNCT/SUNA, sinusitis, acute angle-closure glaucoma, intracranial mass/aneurysm) and explains why cluster headache is most likely based on pattern and autonomic signs.

Addresses familial aspect appropriately (acknowledges increased risk/family history relevance) without overstating inheritance certainty or implying mandatory genetic testing in routine L1 evaluation.
Meets communication milestones: explains diagnosis and, when discussing treatment options, mentions both "oxygen" and "triptan" as standard acute therapies even if not prescribing in this L1 task.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 benchmark (i.e., no treatment WRITE tool calls); if discussing potential future prescriptions, states that allergy, contraindication, and drug-interaction checks are required before prescribing.

If the agent does propose a prescription in narrative, it includes required safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check (especially for triptans/ergots: ischemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment; and for oxygen: caution in severe CO2 retainers), and drug interaction check (e.g., triptans with ergot derivatives within 24 hours; serotonergic agents/MAO inhibitors depending on triptan).
Provides a brief safety-net/return precautions statement (seek urgent care for new neurologic deficits, thunderclap onset, visual loss/eye pain, fever/neck stiffness, or atypical/worsening pattern).

absence of focal neurologic deficits between attacks
red flag screen (thunderclap, fever/meningismus, neuro deficits, age >50 new onset, immunosuppression/cancer, trauma)
steps: Characterize headache (location, severity, duration, frequency, timing/circadian pattern).
Ask about ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms and/or agitation/restlessness during attacks.
Screen for secondary headache red flags and atypical features.
Consider and briefly compare key differentials (migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, paroxysmal hemicrania/SUNCT, sinus/eye pathology).
Explain diagnosis using the term "cluster headache" and note familial history as supportive risk factor.
Discuss standard acute treatments (high-flow oxygen; a triptan such as subcutaneous/intranasal sumatriptan) and mention that safety checks are required before prescribing.
Record diagnosis.

Agent screens for common contributors/secondary causes: sleep schedule/circadian issues, caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, medications/substances, pain, nocturia, depression/anxiety, and symptoms of obstructive sleep apnea (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness) and restless legs syndrome (urge to move legs, worse at rest/night).

Agent avoids unsafe advice: does not recommend combining sedatives with alcohol/opioids; counsels on avoiding driving/operating machinery if sedated.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "cryptogenic organizing pneumonia" and clarifies it is an inflammatory lung condition distinct from typical bacterial pneumonia.

Performs a critical contraindication_check before prescribing systemic steroids (e.g., uncontrolled infection, poorly controlled diabetes, active GI bleeding/ulcer risk without protection, severe psychiatric history, uncontrolled hypertension) and mitigates or defers therapy when contraindications are present.

Provides safety counseling for systemic steroids: infection risk/fever precautions, hyperglycemia symptoms, mood/insomnia, GI upset, blood pressure effects, and advises not to stop abruptly if on a prolonged course (taper plan).

Includes a monitoring and follow-up plan in narrative form even if not required as an outcome tool: reassessment of symptoms and oxygenation, consideration of repeat imaging, and monitoring for steroid adverse effects (glucose, BP, weight).
Uses clear, patient-centered communication and checks understanding/teach-back, including when to seek urgent care (worsening dyspnea, chest pain, hemoptysis, severe hypoxia).
Avoids inappropriate empiric antibiotics as definitive therapy for cryptogenic organizing pneumonia unless there is concurrent evidence of bacterial infection.

Explain infection risk and give clear safety-net instructions (urgent evaluation for fever or worsening symptoms).
Outline immediate evaluation priorities in narrative form (repeat CBC with differential, review meds, assess for infection), without using extra WRITE tools.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose suspected drug-induced neutropenia when history strongly suggests a culprit medication, with the same infection-risk counseling.
steps: Elicit and identify a likely offending medication exposure temporally associated with neutropenia.
Record diagnosis as suspected drug-induced neutropenia.
Explain neutropenia and infection risk; advise urgent care for fever or infection signs.
Recommend prompt clinician review for medication cessation/substitution and confirmatory testing (described narratively only).

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose suspected post-viral/transient neutropenia when there is a recent viral illness and no red flags, while still emphasizing infection precautions.
steps: Elicit recent viral syndrome and absence of high-risk exposures or severe symptoms.
Record diagnosis as suspected transient/post-viral neutropenia.
Explain neutropenia and infection risk; provide clear return precautions (especially fever).
Describe plan for reassessment/repeat blood counts and monitoring (narrative only).
